skill.name: python-executor
skill.description: Run Python code through the inference.sh belt CLI in a sandboxed environment with data, media, scraping, and automation libraries.
skill.summary: Execute Python scripts with preinstalled analysis, media, web, and document libraries.
skill.author: inference-sh-skills

security_audit.summary: Static command findings mostly point to Markdown examples and belt CLI usage, not hidden Ruby backtick execution. The skill intentionally runs arbitrary Python through an external sandbox and includes network-capable examples, so it should publish with a clear execution and network warning.

medium_findings.title: Arbitrary Python Execution Through External CLI
medium_findings.description: The skill is designed to send user-provided Python code to infsh/python-executor through the belt CLI. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but it can run untrusted code and should be restricted to trusted prompts and data.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed tool permits belt commands and the examples pass a code field to infsh/python-executor. The behavior is documented as the main feature, so the risk is real but not malicious by itself.

medium_findings.title: Network-Capable Python Runtime
medium_findings.description: The skill documents HTTP clients, web scraping, and API calls from the executed Python environment. This can be useful, but it may access external sites or transmit data if a user supplies such code.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation explicitly lists HTTP libraries and includes requests examples to public URLs. No credential exfiltration is shown, but network capability is confirmed.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Flagged As Backtick Execution
low_findings.description: The static Ruby or shell backtick findings refer to Markdown inline code and fenced command examples. They are not embedded executable Ruby backticks, although some examples instruct the agent to run belt commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are Markdown documentation and command examples in SKILL.md. No standalone script file or Ruby execution context is present.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Hit Is A Scanner False Positive
low_findings.description: The high blocker at line 3 appears to be triggered by text in the description or URL. No cryptographic algorithm or hashing code is present at that line.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is front matter description text that links to inference.sh. I found no MD5, SHA1, DES, or similar weak algorithm usage there.

low_findings.title: Network Reconnaissance Hit Is Documentation Text
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance finding maps to a note that execution runs in an isolated subprocess. This is descriptive text, not scanning or discovery logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 165 contains the phrase isolated subprocess and no commands for network scanning. The static finding is not supported by semantic context.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash Tool Access For belt Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill declares allowed Bash access for belt commands. This enables external command execution within the specific CLI boundary.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration is explicit and scoped to belt. It is required for the skill but should be visible as an execution risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: User Code Passed To Remote Python App
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples pass a code string to infsh/python-executor. This pattern intentionally executes arbitrary Python in the provider sandbox.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The input schema and examples show direct code submission. The sandbox claim reduces local risk but does not remove remote execution risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: HTTP Requests From Executed Code
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples import requests and contact external URLs. This permits outbound network behavior when user code asks for it.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples contain explicit requests usage with public endpoints. There is no evidence of hidden exfiltration, but the outbound capability is confirmed.

content.user_title: Run Python in a Sandboxed CLI
content.value_statement: Agents often need Python libraries without preparing a local runtime. This skill routes scripts through inference.sh with common analysis, media, scraping, and document tools installed.
actual_capabilities: Runs Python code through infsh/python-executor with the belt CLI.
Uses common libraries such as pandas, NumPy, Matplotlib, requests, Pillow, and OpenCV.
Creates output files in an outputs directory for return to the user.
Supports web scraping, API calls, data analysis, and visualization examples.
Offers default and high-memory runtime variants for larger jobs.
Documents timeout, output capture, and working directory inputs.
limitations: Requires belt CLI access and an inference.sh account or configured environment.
Runs CPU-only workloads and does not provide GPU acceleration.
Execution is non-interactive, so scripts must save files instead of opening displays.
Network use depends on the remote sandbox and target site permissions.

use_cases.title: Analyze CSV Data
use_cases.description: Process tables, calculate summaries, and save charts without setting up a local Python environment.

use_cases.title: Automate Content Processing
use_cases.description: Resize images, generate PDFs, or create short media files from scripted workflows.

use_cases.title: Prototype Scrapers And API Tasks
use_cases.description: Test small web scraping or API scripts in a prepared runtime with HTTP and parsing libraries.

prompt_templates.title: Run A Simple Script
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner test of the Python runtime.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use python-executor to run a short Python script that prints the installed pandas version and returns the output.

prompt_templates.title: Create A Chart
prompt_templates.scenario: Analyze a small dataset and save a visual result.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use python-executor to create a bar chart from this small sales table and save it as an output image.

prompt_templates.title: Scrape A Public Page
prompt_templates.scenario: Extract basic data from a permitted public URL.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use python-executor to fetch this public page, extract its title and headings, and summarize the returned text.

prompt_templates.title: Build A Multi-Step Report
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow with data processing and file output.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use python-executor to clean this dataset, generate summary statistics, create two charts, and save a PDF report.

output_examples.input: A user asks for a quick pandas version check.
output_examples.output: The assistant runs the sandboxed script and returns the printed pandas version with no files attached.

output_examples.input: A user provides a small sales table and asks for a chart.
output_examples.output: The assistant returns a short result summary and an attached chart image saved by the Python script.

output_examples.input: A user asks to inspect a public API response.
output_examples.output: The assistant describes the returned fields and notes the endpoint contacted during execution.

best_practices: Review scripts before execution, especially when they include network requests or file operations.
Save generated artifacts under outputs so the skill can return them reliably.
Set timeouts that match the expected workload and prefer high memory only when needed.
anti_patterns: Do not run code from unknown sources without reviewing its behavior first.
Do not include secrets, credentials, or private tokens in scripts sent to the sandbox.
Do not use this skill for prohibited scraping or requests that violate site terms.

faq.question: Does this run Python locally?
faq.answer: No. It uses the belt CLI to run code through the inference.sh Python executor.

faq.question: Can it access the internet?
faq.answer: The examples show HTTP requests from executed code. Treat outbound network use as possible.

faq.question: Can it return generated files?
faq.answer: Yes. Files saved under outputs are documented as automatically returned.

faq.question: Does it support GPU workloads?
faq.answer: No. The documented environment is CPU-only.

faq.question: Is it safe to run untrusted code?
faq.answer: No. Review code first and avoid secrets, because the skill executes user-provided Python.

faq.question: Why is the risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill has legitimate purpose, but it combines external commands, arbitrary code execution, and network-capable libraries.
